question: According to the lecture slides shown visually, what course and lecture number is this material from, and what specific cache design does the slide about 'Internal Cache Organization' focus on first before moving to set-associative caches?
answer: The lecture is from MIT 6.004 Fall 2018, specifically Lecture 14 (L14) titled 'Caches 2.' The 'Internal Cache Organization' slide first focuses on the Direct-Mapped Cache, which divides a 32-bit address into Tag, Index, and Offset fields, before moving on to the n-way Set-associative Cache.

ground_truth_spans.description: Frame caption identifies course as MIT 6.004 Fall 2018 L14 Caches 2
Frame caption identifies the Direct-Mapped Cache as the first topic in Internal Cache Organization

question: The lecture visually shows a slide on 'Stores' discussing write policies. What two write-hit policies are shown on that slide, and how does the lecture explain the motivation for preferring write-back in terms of bandwidth?
answer: The 'Stores' slide shows two write-hit policies: the write-back policy, which writes only to the cache and updates the next level memory when the line is evicted, and the write-through policy, which writes to both the cache and the next level memory simultaneously. The lecture explains that write-back saves bandwidth because you do not have to write to main memory every single time, since you might just be overwriting the same location again shortly after.

ground_truth_spans.description: Frame caption of Stores slide listing write-back and write-through policies
Transcript explanation that write-back saves bandwidth by not writing to main memory every time

question: The slide titled 'Replacement Policy' is shown visually. What does it say about replacement choices for a direct-mapped cache, and how does the transcript explain the LRU algorithm's motivation for set-associative caches?
answer: The 'Replacement Policy' slide states that for a direct-mapped cache there is no choice because each address maps to exactly one cache line. For set-associative caches, the transcript explains that Least Recently Used (LRU) is the most common algorithm, motivated by the locality principle: if something was used recently it is likely to be used again, so evicting the least recently used item minimizes future misses.

ground_truth_spans.description: Frame caption of Replacement Policy slide stating direct-mapped cache has no choice
Transcript explaining LRU motivation based on locality principle

question: The lecture slide shown visually is titled 'Blocking vs. Non-Blocking cache.' What properties does each type have according to the slide caption, and what does the transcript say about which type will be implemented in the class?
answer: According to the slide caption, a blocking cache has at most one outstanding miss, must wait for memory to respond, and does not accept processor requests in the meantime. A non-blocking cache can handle multiple outstanding requests simultaneously. The transcript states that non-blocking caches are much more complex, so the class will focus on and implement blocking caches.

ground_truth_spans.description: Frame caption listing blocking cache properties: one outstanding miss, wait for memory, no new requests
Transcript stating non-blocking is more complex and class will focus on blocking caches

question: The lecture shows a slide titled 'Cached Memory Systems.' What components are shown in the diagram according to the frame caption, and what does the transcript say about the difference between the word requests made by the processor and the line requests made by the SRAM to DRAM?
answer: The 'Cached Memory Systems' slide diagram shows a processor with a register file (rf) and program counter (pc) connected to an SRAM-based cache, which in turn is connected to a DRAM-based memory via lineReq and lineResp signals. The transcript explains that processor requests are word requests providing a 32-bit address and expecting a 32-bit response, whereas SRAM requests to DRAM are line requests fetching 16 words (64 bytes) at a time.

ground_truth_spans.description: Frame caption of Cached Memory Systems diagram showing processor, SRAM cache, DRAM memory, lineReq, lineResp
Transcript distinguishing 32-bit word requests from processor vs 16-word line requests from SRAM to DRAM

question: A slide titled 'Cache Interface' is shown visually in the lecture. According to the frame caption, how many methods does the cache interface have and what are they, and what does the transcript say about which methods are processor-side versus back-end side?
answer: According to the frame caption and transcript, the cache interface has four methods: req (request) and resp (response) on the processor side, and lineReq (line request) and lineResp (line response) on the back-end DRAM side. The transcript explains that the first two methods are how the processor communicates with the cache, while the other two are how the cache makes requests to and accepts responses from main memory.

ground_truth_spans.description: Frame caption of Cache Interface slide showing req, resp, lineReq, lineResp
Transcript explaining processor-side methods vs back-end DRAM-side methods

question: The frame caption of a slide in this lecture shows a 'Hit and miss performance' slide. What latency is described for a cache hit, and what two different miss latency formulas are given for the no-evacuation and evacuation cases?
answer: According to the 'Hit and miss performance' slide, a cache hit has a 1-cycle latency with appropriate hitQ design, directly related to the latency of the L1 cache. For a miss with no evacuation (no dirty line to write back), the latency is DRAM load latency plus 2 times SRAM latency. For a miss requiring evacuation (dirty line must be written back), the latency is DRAM store latency plus DRAM load latency plus 2 times SRAM latency.

ground_truth_spans.description: Frame caption showing hit: 1-cycle latency with hitQ design
Frame caption showing miss no evacuation: DRAM load + 2x SRAM, and evacuation: DRAM store + DRAM load + 2x SRAM

question: According to the visual slide caption for the 'Cache Organization' slide, what three arrays make up the cache-array unit shown in the diagram?
answer: According to the frame caption for the 'Cache Organization' slide, the cache-array unit diagram shows three arrays: a Data Array (represented by yellow rectangles), a Tag Array (represented by orange rectangles), and a Status Array (represented by blue rectangles).

ground_truth_spans.description: Frame caption describing cache-array unit with data array, tag array, and status array

question: According to the frame caption visible in the lecture, what specific code is shown on the slide for the 'Blocking cache' module definition?
answer: According to the frame caption, the 'Blocking cache' slide shows the module definition `module mkBlockingCache(Cache#(LogNLines));` along with the instantiation of a Cache Array Unit as `CAU#(LogNLines) cau <- mkCAU();` and a FIFO for the hit queue as `FIFO#(Word) hitQ`.

ground_truth_spans.description: Frame caption showing mkBlockingCache module definition with CAU instantiation and hitQ FIFO

question: The lecture explains the three possible states of a cache line needed to support write-back caches. What are those three states and what does each mean? Also, what is the Miss-allocate policy described later for handling write misses?
answer: The three states for a cache line under a write-back policy are: Invalid (data is not valid), Clean (data is valid but has not been modified, so it is consistent with main memory), and Dirty (data is valid but has been modified, so it is not consistent with main memory). The Miss-allocate policy means that on a write miss, the cache always brings the missing line into the cache before performing the write, rather than writing directly to main memory.

ground_truth_spans.description: Transcript explaining invalid, clean, dirty states for write-back cache support
Transcript explaining miss-allocate policy: bring line into cache on write miss

question: The lecture describes the DRAM interface and the SRAM interface separately. What fixed line size does the class assume for DRAM accesses, and how does the SRAM interface differ in that it allows the user to specify its size?
answer: The class assumes a fixed DRAM line size of 64 bytes, which corresponds to 16 words fetched at a time from main memory. The SRAM interface differs because it allows the user to specify the number of index bits when instantiating it, which determines how many lines the cache holds (2 to the power of the number of index bits), and also allows specification of the data type it stores.

ground_truth_spans.description: Transcript stating DRAM fixed size is 64 bytes, 16 words fetched at a time
Transcript explaining SRAM allows specifying number of index bits to determine cache size

question: The lecture describes what happens in the blocking cache's FSM when the cache-array unit returns a Miss. What two additional states must be traversed, and what specifically happens in the waitDRAMResponse state when the original request was a load versus a store?
answer: When the cache-array unit returns a Miss, the blocking cache FSM must traverse two additional states: SendRequest (to fetch the needed data from DRAM) and WaitDRAMResponse. In the WaitDRAMResponse state, if the original request was a load, the cache extracts the specific word at the word-offset from the returned 16-word line and places it in the hit queue for the processor. If the original request was a store, the cache modifies the returned line at the word-offset with the data to be written, then updates the cache-array unit with that modified line and marks its status as dirty.

ground_truth_spans.description: Transcript describing SendRequest and WaitDRAMResponse as the two additional miss states
Transcript explaining load vs store handling in waitDRAMResponse state

question: In the lecture, what specific reason is given for why a 2-way set-associative cache can implement LRU replacement using just one bit of state per cache line?
answer: The lecture states that for a 2-way set-associative cache, you can implement Least Recently Used replacement by adding just one bit of state to each of your cache lines. This single bit is sufficient because with only two ways, you only need to track which of the two ways was used most recently, which is a binary choice requiring exactly one bit.

ground_truth_spans.description: Transcript explaining 2-way set-associative LRU implemented with one bit per cache line

question: In the lecture's description of the cache-array unit response type, what are the three fields included in the CAUResp data type and what information does each carry?
answer: The CAUResp data type includes three fields: first, a hitMiss field that indicates whether the result was a load hit, a store hit, or a miss; second, a load value field (ldValue) that carries the specific word returned when there is a load hit; and third, a tag-line field that carries the entire evicted cache line when there is a miss, so that if the line is dirty it can be written back to main memory.

ground_truth_spans.description: Transcript describing CAUResp with hitMiss enum, ldValue word, and tag-line for miss case
